items.message: I have completed 1 task and got a rating of 4, but its says task limit reached, can i get unthrottled now?

Hey team! 👋

Today's [**Stargazer Axiom daily thread**](https://community.outlier.ai/t/daily-thread-07-11/2903684) is up.

Please take a few minutes to read it before tasking today. The topic is **Rubric Quality**, with a reminder about **One criterion = one thing. AND = split.**.

Quick rule: If your criterion has AND, split it into two.



Use the daily thread for blockers, Cursor issues, validation/eval problems, onboarding questions, or task-related doubts 🙌

decisions.message: I have completed 1 task and got a rating of 4, but its says task limit reached, can i get unthrottled now?
decisions.reason: Project memory clearly states that the 1-task/24hr throttle is automatic with no exceptions until approximately 3 tasks are completed, at which point quality metrics are evaluated. No individual task score or user request removes it early.
decisions.reply: The 1-task per 24-hour throttle applies automatically until you complete about 3 tasks, at which point the team will review your quality metrics to decide on more volume. Your rating of 4 on your first task is great progress, so keep working and you'll be unthrottled once you reach that milestone.
guidelineSnippets:
Task Overview
Description
Onboarding &
Installations
Before tasking for the first time, you must complete the project
onboarding and reach out to the project team after completing your
quality assessment. Then you will set up Cursor and Docker. You
only need to do this once!
Setup and
Analysis
First, you will clone the assigned repo and set up your environment.
You will also explore the codebase so that you understand its
structure and functionality.
Issue Creation
and Solution
You will write a prompt that asks the agent to make changes to the
codebase based on an assigned issue (e.g., bug fixes or new
features). You will make sure that your prompt is challenging
enough that at least one model fails to solve it. Then, you will write
your own Golden Solution to your prompt.
Testing
You will create unit tests that can check whether a given solution
adds all of the requested functionality without breaking any
previously existing functionality.
Docker and
Scripts
You will prepare Dockerfiles and scripts that ensure that your work
on the task is reproducible.
Validation Script You will run a validation script that checks that your solution passes
all of the unit tests that you wrote.
Add Tags You will add relevant tags to your task to categorize it.
Rubric Creation You will write a rubric that consists of a series of criteria that can
grade how good or bad a response to your prompt is.
Evaluation Script
Before submission, you will run an evaluation script on your task.
The suite checks the quality and alignment of your prompt, tests,
rubrics, gold patch, Dockerfiles, and granularity.
Step 0 Onboarding & Installations (return to TOC
Project Onboarding (return to TOC
Before your first task, you will be guided through our onboarding process, where you will
learn the basics of what you need to know to succeed on the project.

As part of the onboarding process, you will submit a quality assessment.
Please reach out to the project team in the Zoom War Room after completing the quality
assessment. This step is mandatory for you to begin tasking. The project team will need
to perform a manual review to ensure that you passed the quality assessment and to
provide access to Cursor.
Environment Setup (return to TOC
Before getting started with the project, both Cursor and Docker must be properly set up
and running. Here is a quick guide to make sure your environment is properly set up.
Cursor
● Follow this link and download the 2.6 cursor version. If you already have Cursor
installed, uninstall it and install this specific version, without this version, the models
wonʼt work.
● Even if you already have access to the Cursor Team Plan, reach out to the Zoom
War Room and ask any of the QMs for a new plan, as it must be renewed for this
project.
● Go to the Key Manager: https://cursor-intelligence-service.outlier.ai/key-manager
● Click on View API Key
● Open Cursor Settings → Models
● Disable all models, and add:
○ claude-qwen3527b-scale
○ claude-4.6-sonnet-scale
● Enable OpenAI API Key; paste in your API Key and Override Base URL with
https://cursor-intelligence-api.outlier.ai/api/v1
● Click "Verify".
● Test these models in Cursor now.
Docker
● Make sure Docker is installed and up to date. If you don't have it yet, follow this
guide.
● Ensure Docker has enough RAM and disk space to run containers smoothly.
● Clean up unused images and containers regularly to free up space and avoid build
errors.

If itʼs not bug injection, please upload an empty basetoinstance.patch file to the task
Please note that the Outlier platform does not allow literally empty files. Adding an empty
line or a space is acceptable)
2.4 Test the Issue Message (return to TOC
Make sure the issue message is challenging enough to cause at least one model to fail.
To do so, open only the App/ folder in Cursor and have both models available: Sonnet 4.6
and Qwen 3.5 27b, if you donʼt have them available go to the Environment Setup section
in the appendix to see a full guide on how to set them up.
● If it is a Bug injection task, make sure the app/ folder contains the bug
● Paste the issue message and select one model (Sonnet 4.6 or Qwen 3.5 27b). Then
check whether it fails to create a valid solution.
● Repeat the same with the other model.
● If both models solve the issue correctly, rewrite or adjust the issue message to
increase its complexity or change the issue topic until one of them fails.
● Ensure that the reported failure is directly tied to a specific requirement described in
the issue message. The model should only fail when it does not correctly implement
an expected behavior explicitly requested in the prompt, not because of
assumptions, inferred expectations, or invented edge cases that are not part of the
original issue description. If you want to know about valid failures refer to the
Sample Invalid Model Failures section.
● When at least one model fails, and the issue message meets all requirements, select
"Yes" and continue with the task.
● Extract the model failure trace from Cursor as documentation. If both models fail,
capturing one of them is enough). If you need a guide on how to generate that trace,
go to the How to generate the model trace section.
● Generate a model_trace.patch containing the exact changes made by the model
during its trace. If you need a guide on how to generate that file, go to the How to
generate model_trace.patch section.
IMPORTANT Ensure that only the App/ folder is opened in Cursor so the
models do not have access to the patches with the solution (you can open a
new Cursor window with just the App/ content).

1.2 Clone Repo & Analyze Codebase (return to TOC
First, open a new terminal and clone the given task repo using the provided repository
clone command. Rename the cloned folder as App/. After that, explore the codebase you
cloned:
● Review the code structure and architecture
● Understand dependencies and build process
● Understand the issue type you were assigned for this task Review Issue Types
Examples section in the Appendix for specific examples):
○ New feature request: Adding a new functionality not currently in the given
code.
○ Bug injection + resolution: Introducing a breaking change to working code
○ Migration: Adapting all or part of the existing code to a new
library/pattern/version without losing its original functionality.
○ Performance Optimization: Improve speed, efficiency, or resource usage with
no changes to the original functionality.
○ Maintenance or testing enhancement: Updating dependencies, tests, or tooling
Step 2 Issue Creation and Solution (return to TOC
2.1 Write Issue Statement (return to TOC
After reviewing the codebase, you will create a prompt based on the issue type that you
were assigned. The prompt should make a request to the agent based on your issue type.
It should also be complex enough that it challenges the models (Sonnet 4.6 and Qwen
3.5 27b) so that one of them fails. You can see examples of the different issue types in
Issue Types Examples section in the Appendix.
Prompt Guidelines:
● Specific enough that it is possible for the model to succeed
● Not so detailed that it gives away the solution to the model

guidelineSnippets:
● Base Dockerfile (base.Dockerfile): This Dockerfile sets up the environment by
creating /app, cloning the repository into it, and installing any required system or
project dependencies.
● Instance Dockerfile (instance.Dockerfile): This Dockerfile extends the base image
and applies patches/basetoinstance.patch to /app. The build logic must handle
the patch file, whether it is populated (this will be the case in Bug Injection tasks) or
empty (this will be the case in all other tasks). In the case of Bug injection, the patch
serves to transition the codebase into the designated problem state for evaluation.
3. Scripts
● The scripts/ directory contains executable shell scripts (run_script.sh,
parse_results.sh) that are mounted into the container under /app/scripts.
These scripts are responsible for applying additional patches, running tests, and
parsing results.
4. Patches
● The patches/ directory defines all transformations applied to the repository at
/app.
○ basetoinstance.patch modifies the cloned repository to represent the
problem scenario.
○ test_patch.patch introduces tests that should fail before your patch is
applied.
○ gold_patch.patch introduces your solution to the issue, which should pass all
the unit tests.
5. JSONFormatted Tests Results
● The test_results.json output from parse_results.sh
Step 1 Setup and Analysis (return to TOC
1.1 Environment Setup (return to TOC
Before reviewing the assigned codebase, make sure your environment is set up, Cursor is
configured, and Docker is running. Review Environment Setup section in Step 0 for
detailed setup instructions.
Create the following folders in your working directory:

● Think ahead. Before writing, plan how the gold solution and tests will look. If you
cannot picture them, the prompt is not ready.
2.2 Rate the difficulty of your issue (return to TOC
Rate your issue by counting how many of the following complexity signals apply:
1. Spread: The fix changes 3 or more files.
2. Cross-module interaction: The fix affects more than one part of the project.
3. Architectural awareness: To fix it, you need to understand how the project is
organized.
4. Non-trivial logic: The fix needs decision-making in the code, not just renaming,
moving, or deleting something.
5. Implicit edge cases: The solution should also handle special cases or possible
errors.
6. Regression risk: A bad fix could accidentally break something else.
Difficulty Signals that apply
Easy 1 - 2
Medium 3 - 4
Hard 5
2.3 Create the Issue (return to TOC
Location: App/
● For all issue types except Bug Injection, the files inside app/ must remain identical
to the original repo.
● For Bug Injection, introduce the bug directly inside the app/ folder so that this
modified version represents the faulty instance state that the solution will fix.
2.3.1 Generate Base-to-Instance Patch
You must generate an additional patch called basetoinstance.patch. If the issue type is
Bug Injection, after introducing the bug, you need to capture the exact diff between the
working base state and the broken instance state

5. Run cleanly: The script should complete successfully using the expected flow,

IMPORTANT: All the repository dependencies and the necessary assets
(env variables, etc) should be installed/configured in the
base.Dockerfile.
This also includes extra test-only dependencies that are needed to run
the tests, even if they are not included in the original repository and are
not added by the gold patch. For example, if the tests need playwright,
it and anything else required for it to run must be set up in the
base.Dockerfile.
The run_script.sh should be focused ONLY on applying the patches
and running the tests for each phase.
It must not install ANY dependencies or run npm install (or similar).
4.2 Create Parse Script (return to TOC
● Location: scripts/parse_results.sh
● Template: Use the parse_results.sh template in the Templates document and match
the run_script.sh output.
● The script should:
1. Parse test output from the run_script.sh
2. Identify Phase 1 results (broken state)
3. Identify Phase 2 results (fixed state)
4. Determine the overall result. Should output “SUCCESSˮ if all F2P failed and all
P2P passed in phase 1, and all F2P/P2P passed in phase 2.
5. Output results to a JSON file test_results.json with the required output
format

guidelineSnippets:
Task Overview
Description
Onboarding &
Installations
Before tasking for the first time, you must complete the project
onboarding and reach out to the project team after completing your
quality assessment. Then you will set up Cursor and Docker. You
only need to do this once!
Setup and
Analysis
First, you will clone the assigned repo and set up your environment.
You will also explore the codebase so that you understand its
structure and functionality.
Issue Creation
and Solution
You will write a prompt that asks the agent to make changes to the
codebase based on an assigned issue (e.g., bug fixes or new
features). You will make sure that your prompt is challenging
enough that at least one model fails to solve it. Then, you will write
your own Golden Solution to your prompt.
Testing
You will create unit tests that can check whether a given solution
adds all of the requested functionality without breaking any
previously existing functionality.
Docker and
Scripts
You will prepare Dockerfiles and scripts that ensure that your work
on the task is reproducible.
Validation Script You will run a validation script that checks that your solution passes
all of the unit tests that you wrote.
Add Tags You will add relevant tags to your task to categorize it.
Rubric Creation You will write a rubric that consists of a series of criteria that can
grade how good or bad a response to your prompt is.
Evaluation Script
Before submission, you will run an evaluation script on your task.
The suite checks the quality and alignment of your prompt, tests,
rubrics, gold patch, Dockerfiles, and granularity.

assessment. This step is mandatory for you to begin tasking. The project team will need

Before getting started with the project, both Cursor and Docker must be properly set up
and running. Here is a quick guide to make sure your environment is properly set up.
Cursor
● Follow this link and download the 2.6 cursor version. If you already have Cursor

wonʼt work.

project.

● Click "Verify".
● Test these models in Cursor now.
Docker
● Make sure Docker is installed and up to date. If you don't have it yet, follow this
guide.
● Ensure Docker has enough RAM and disk space to run containers smoothly.
● Clean up unused images and containers regularly to free up space and avoid build
errors.

Step 7 Rubric Creation (return to TOC
A rubric is a set of criteria that can be used to grade objectively and granularly how good
a response is to a given prompt. You will create a series of criteria that exhaustively cover
all the features of an ideal response to the prompt that you created. These criteria should
cover all the features of a good response without being so restrictive that they exclude
other possible good solutions. Therefore, you can start with your Golden Solution as a
model for the rubric criteria, but make sure that you donʼt write them in such a way that
other possible good implementations are left out.
Guidelines (return to TOC
● For tips, tricks, and examples, consult the Tips and Tricks for Creating Rubrics
section in the Appendix!
● You may write as many criteria as needed.
● Each criterion should match one (or more) parts of the prompt. If your criterion does
not have its counterpart in the prompt, it is most likely that it is over-specific or
ambiguous.
● There may be zero, one, or multiple criteria per quality dimension.
● You will assign weights to each criterion based on how important it is to the issue
that you created.

Step 8 Evaluation Script (return to TOC
Before submitting, you must run your task through the evaluation suite to catch issues the
validation script cannot detect. The suite checks the quality and alignment of your
prompt, tests, rubrics, gold patch, Dockerfiles, and granularity.
Download the evaluation suite here: Stargazer_Eval.zip
How to run it (return to TOC
● Unzip Stargazer_Eval.zip into your task's root directory.
Run the Stargazer Eval
● Open the Guide/ folder and follow the HOW_TO_RUN_EVAL.md guidelines.
● Review the consolidated report. The summary table shows a PASS/FAIL verdict for
each component: Prompt, Tests, Rubrics, Alignment, Granularity, Dockerfile, and
Golden Patch.
● If any component returns FAIL, use the “How to Fixˮ table in the report to address
every failed check, then re-run the master eval.
○ Note: If you modify any of the previously uploaded files (e.g.,
test_patch.patch), make sure you also re-run the validation script and
upload the new files
● Only submit your task once the overall verdict is PASS and all components are
review-ready.
This step is mandatory! A task that has not been evaluated with the Stargazer suite is
considered incomplete and may be rejected during review.

decisions.message: I have completed 1 task and got a rating of 4, but its says task limit reached, can i get unthrottled now?
decisions.reason: Project memory clearly states that the 1-task/24hr throttle is automatic with no exceptions until approximately 3 tasks are completed, at which point quality metrics are evaluated. No individual task score or user request removes it early.
decisions.reply: The 1-task per 24-hour throttle applies automatically until you complete about 3 tasks, at which point the team will review your quality metrics to decide on more volume. Your rating of 4 on your first task is great progress, so keep working and you'll be unthrottled once you reach that milestone.

guidelineSnippets:
● Base Dockerfile (base.Dockerfile): This Dockerfile sets up the environment by
creating /app, cloning the repository into it, and installing any required system or
project dependencies.
● Instance Dockerfile (instance.Dockerfile): This Dockerfile extends the base image
and applies patches/basetoinstance.patch to /app. The build logic must handle
the patch file, whether it is populated (this will be the case in Bug Injection tasks) or
empty (this will be the case in all other tasks). In the case of Bug injection, the patch
serves to transition the codebase into the designated problem state for evaluation.
3. Scripts
● The scripts/ directory contains executable shell scripts (run_script.sh,
parse_results.sh) that are mounted into the container under /app/scripts.
These scripts are responsible for applying additional patches, running tests, and
parsing results.
4. Patches
● The patches/ directory defines all transformations applied to the repository at
/app.
○ basetoinstance.patch modifies the cloned repository to represent the
problem scenario.
○ test_patch.patch introduces tests that should fail before your patch is
applied.
○ gold_patch.patch introduces your solution to the issue, which should pass all
the unit tests.
5. JSONFormatted Tests Results
● The test_results.json output from parse_results.sh

● Think ahead. Before writing, plan how the gold solution and tests will look. If you
cannot picture them, the prompt is not ready.
2.2 Rate the difficulty of your issue (return to TOC
Rate your issue by counting how many of the following complexity signals apply:
1. Spread: The fix changes 3 or more files.
2. Cross-module interaction: The fix affects more than one part of the project.
3. Architectural awareness: To fix it, you need to understand how the project is
organized.
4. Non-trivial logic: The fix needs decision-making in the code, not just renaming,
moving, or deleting something.
5. Implicit edge cases: The solution should also handle special cases or possible
errors.
6. Regression risk: A bad fix could accidentally break something else.
Difficulty Signals that apply
Easy 1 - 2
Medium 3 - 4
Hard 5
2.3 Create the Issue (return to TOC
Location: App/
● For all issue types except Bug Injection, the files inside app/ must remain identical
to the original repo.
● For Bug Injection, introduce the bug directly inside the app/ folder so that this
modified version represents the faulty instance state that the solution will fix.
2.3.1 Generate Base-to-Instance Patch
You must generate an additional patch called basetoinstance.patch. If the issue type is
Bug Injection, after introducing the bug, you need to capture the exact diff between the
working base state and the broken instance state

Task Overview
Description
Onboarding &
Installations
Before tasking for the first time, you must complete the project
onboarding and reach out to the project team after completing your
quality assessment. Then you will set up Cursor and Docker. You
only need to do this once!
Setup and
Analysis
First, you will clone the assigned repo and set up your environment.
You will also explore the codebase so that you understand its
structure and functionality.
Issue Creation
and Solution
You will write a prompt that asks the agent to make changes to the
codebase based on an assigned issue (e.g., bug fixes or new
features). You will make sure that your prompt is challenging
enough that at least one model fails to solve it. Then, you will write
your own Golden Solution to your prompt.
Testing
You will create unit tests that can check whether a given solution
adds all of the requested functionality without breaking any
previously existing functionality.
Docker and
Scripts
You will prepare Dockerfiles and scripts that ensure that your work
on the task is reproducible.
Validation Script You will run a validation script that checks that your solution passes
all of the unit tests that you wrote.
Add Tags You will add relevant tags to your task to categorize it.
Rubric Creation You will write a rubric that consists of a series of criteria that can
grade how good or bad a response to your prompt is.
Evaluation Script
Before submission, you will run an evaluation script on your task.
The suite checks the quality and alignment of your prompt, tests,
rubrics, gold patch, Dockerfiles, and granularity.

Please reach out to the project team in the Zoom War Room after completing the quality

Environment Setup (return to TOC
Before getting started with the project, both Cursor and Docker must be properly set up
and running. Here is a quick guide to make sure your environment is properly set up.
Cursor
● Follow this link and download the 2.6 cursor version. If you already have Cursor
installed, uninstall it and install this specific version, without this version, the models
wonʼt work.
● Even if you already have access to the Cursor Team Plan, reach out to the Zoom
War Room and ask any of the QMs for a new plan, as it must be renewed for this
project.
● Go to the Key Manager: https://cursor-intelligence-service.outlier.ai/key-manager
● Click on View API Key

Step 7 Rubric Creation (return to TOC
A rubric is a set of criteria that can be used to grade objectively and granularly how good
a response is to a given prompt. You will create a series of criteria that exhaustively cover
all the features of an ideal response to the prompt that you created. These criteria should
cover all the features of a good response without being so restrictive that they exclude
other possible good solutions. Therefore, you can start with your Golden Solution as a
model for the rubric criteria, but make sure that you donʼt write them in such a way that
other possible good implementations are left out.
Guidelines (return to TOC
● For tips, tricks, and examples, consult the Tips and Tricks for Creating Rubrics
section in the Appendix!
● You may write as many criteria as needed.
● Each criterion should match one (or more) parts of the prompt. If your criterion does
not have its counterpart in the prompt, it is most likely that it is over-specific or
ambiguous.
● There may be zero, one, or multiple criteria per quality dimension.
● You will assign weights to each criterion based on how important it is to the issue
that you created.

Step 8 Evaluation Script (return to TOC
Before submitting, you must run your task through the evaluation suite to catch issues the
validation script cannot detect. The suite checks the quality and alignment of your
prompt, tests, rubrics, gold patch, Dockerfiles, and granularity.
Download the evaluation suite here: Stargazer_Eval.zip
How to run it (return to TOC
● Unzip Stargazer_Eval.zip into your task's root directory.
Run the Stargazer Eval
● Open the Guide/ folder and follow the HOW_TO_RUN_EVAL.md guidelines.
● Review the consolidated report. The summary table shows a PASS/FAIL verdict for
each component: Prompt, Tests, Rubrics, Alignment, Granularity, Dockerfile, and
Golden Patch.
● If any component returns FAIL, use the “How to Fixˮ table in the report to address
every failed check, then re-run the master eval.
○ Note: If you modify any of the previously uploaded files (e.g.,
test_patch.patch), make sure you also re-run the validation script and
upload the new files
● Only submit your task once the overall verdict is PASS and all components are
review-ready.
This step is mandatory! A task that has not been evaluated with the Stargazer suite is
considered incomplete and may be rejected during review.
